text: Thick fibrous adhesions are visible in the surgical field, indicating dense connective tissue formations.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the small bowel, lifting it out of the pelvis to expose and identify the rectum.
The video focuses on the pelvic dissection during the colectomy, showing the surgeon carefully navigating through thick fibrous adhesions in the pelvic region.
The surgical view focuses on the right pelvic side wall, where a surgical clip is visible.
The surgeon identifies the iliorectal anastomosis site, which becomes apparent despite initial obscurity during this stage of the colectomy.

The surgeon attempts to find a dissection plane in the pelvis, encountering dense adhesions. The external iliac artery is visible being drawn up onto the rectum, and the surgeon sweeps it down, revealing the absence of a normal tissue plane.
The surgeon attempts to unfold and separate tissue adhesions in the pelvic region, searching for a viable plane to continue the dissection.
The surgeon examines the right lateral aspect of the rectum, assessing the retro rectal plane for any abnormalities or disruptions from prior surgeries.
The surgeon carefully navigates the dissection towards the sacral promontory, attempting to access the space over this anatomical landmark.
The surgeon identifies the right ureter, which is visibly drawn up onto the rectum, with the iliac vessel also apparent in the surgical field.

The surgeon gently sweeps tissue down, revealing the right ureter without the aid of stents or ICG, which aids in identification. The right ureter is clearly visible, providing reassurance, while the left ureter is anticipated to be more challenging to visualize.
The surgeon continues dissection, working downwards to locate the sacral promontory or find an entry point into the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon uses the right ureter as a lateral boundary while working anteriorly to identify potential anastomosis sites.

The surgeon navigates the pelvic region between the bladder and bowel, encountering significant adhesions and scarring in the expected natural planes. The tissue appears fibrosed, suggesting potential retroperitoneal fibrosis.

The surgeon navigates to the left anterior side, working along the edge of the bowel in a fused plane likely due to previous surgery. The surgeon then returns to the right side, working higher over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon visually identifies the ureter, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the rectal mesentery and continues dissection, using the right ureter as a lateral boundary to guide the process.

The surgeon navigates towards the pelvis, identifying what appears to be the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, which is notably scarred. The right common iliac artery and its bifurcation into the external and internal iliac arteries are visible, as well as the left common iliac vein. The posterior window of the TME plane and the walled iris fascia are also observed.
The surgeon identifies the bladder while attempting to follow the dissection plane down into the pelvis, exploring the anterior region.
The surgeon works down the left-hand side, attempting to separate tissue from the retroperitoneum. The iliac vessels are visible, pulsating on the left side of the screen.

The surgeon encounters a large wad of fat between the bowel and the iliac, struggling to identify clear anatomical planes. The tissue appears dense, potentially consisting of lymph nodes or residual colon mesentery from previous surgery.

The surgeon continues dissection from known to unknown areas, identifying the anterior surface of the rectum. They proceed with medial to lateral dissection beneath the retrorectal space on the patient's right side.

The surgeon continues dissection along the right total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, with the ureter visibly elevated as a lateral boundary. The mesorectal fascia and walled iris fascia are identified posterior to the sacral promontory. The hypogastric plexus nerves are visible, being swept down posteriorly on the right side.
The surgeon works posteriorly along the sacrum, identifying a normal appearing plane. The dissection is extended laterally on both the right and left sides to facilitate easier lateral dissection.

The surgeon attempts to dissect posteriorly, working in a medial to lateral fashion on the left-hand side. The tissue planes appear fused, complicating the dissection. The goal is to create a window or locate the ureter to facilitate the challenging lateral dissection.

The surgeon navigates the posterior plane, visualizing the ureter elevated in the surgical field. They carefully dissect through the right-hand side, leaving some mesorectum on the right side wall to avoid proximity to the ureter as it approaches the bladder. The dissection progresses towards the lateral TME plane on the right.
The surgeon performs an anterior dissection beneath the prostate and seminal vesicles, navigating through a fused plane while constantly checking known landmarks and areas of safety.

The surgeon attempts lateral dissection on the left side of the rectum, encountering a large mass of fat. The surgeon carefully navigates between the bowel wall and the fat, aiming to connect with the previously dissected medial plane.

The surgeon works from a medial to lateral fashion, attempting to locate the ureter while identifying the left common iliac vein and artery. A sponge is placed over the vessels to facilitate dissection in the left mesorectum posteriorly. The surgeon clamps proximal to the sewn anastomosis, using a flexible sigmoidoscope to inspect the rectum, which is filled with polyps, obscuring normal mucosa.
The surgeon navigates proximally into the small bowel, observing extensive polyposis throughout. The area where the polyps cease is marked, and surgical clips are applied to this region.

The surgeon encounters a highly tethered small bowel and performs a lengthy laparoscopic lysis of adhesions. The suspected end-to-side anastomosis with a long blind limb is exteriorized through a Pfannenstiel incision to facilitate further adhesion removal.

The surgeon performs a lateral dissection into the retroperitoneum, identifying a safe window down the left-sided TME plane. The large wad of fat is removed with the specimen, while the ureter is visible and positioned laterally, ensuring safety during dissection. The thick and fibrous tissue requires multiple burns and cuts with the ligature to progress, with frequent checks on the ureter's position.

The surgeon completes the dissection from the abdominal side, achieving better exposure of the surgical field. The anatomical structures are clearly visible, ensuring a safe continuation of the procedure.

The surgeon performs a perineal dissection from a lithotomy position, while a partner continues residual lysis of adhesions through a fan and steel incision, disconnecting the ileum at the level of the clip.

The surgeon exteriorizes the specimen through the stoma site, revealing the fan and steel incision. The end of the ileum is visible, suspended in the wound with a suture. The surgeon inspects the pelvic area to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon confirms hemostasis in the pelvic region following the completion of the abdominoperineal resection (APR). Surgical gauze is visible from the perineal side, and the right ureter appears skeletonized. The ileum is prepared for exteriorization to create an ileostomy.

The surgeon checks the mesentery for flatness and ensures there is no twisting as the end of the ileum is brought to the right lower quadrant port site for ileostomy creation. The ileostomy is pulled out and positioned with the mesentery oriented towards the head and the bowel towards the feet, while spinning the bowel to confirm there is no twist.
